1: 'And Job again took up his discourse' — wayyōsep̄ ʾîyyôḇ śēʾt mĕšālô. The word māšāl (discourse/parable/proverb) is the formal wisdom-literature genre marker: it governs the wisdom sayings of Proverbs (Job 1:1; Job 10:1; Job 25:1) and the longer discourses in Job. By designating his speech a māšāl, Job claims the authoritative form of the wisdom sage. The 'again' (wayyōsep̄ = he continued/repeated) may mark the resumption after a gap — Job 26 ended Job's response to Bildad, and Job 27 begins a new section that will extend through Job 31. The structural position: this opens Job's final extended defense (Job 27-31), the climactic self-assessment before the divine speech.
2: Job swears an oath 'as God lives' (ḥay-ʾēl) despite God being the very one who has 'denied him justice' (hēsîr mišpāṭî) and 'made him bitter' (hēmar nap̄šî). This is one of the most theologically striking moves in the book: Job invokes the name of his adversary as the ground of his oath. In the OT, oath-formulas 'as YHWH lives' (ḥay-YHWH) are standard judicial forms affirming commitment to truth (1 Samuel 14:39,45; Ruth 3:13; Jeremiah 4:2). Job continues to use God as the ground of truth-telling even when he experiences God as his opponent — preserving theological integrity while maintaining legal complaint.
3: 'As long as my breath (nišmāṯî) remains and God's breath (rûaḥ ʾĕlôah) is in my nostrils' — the verse identifies two aspects of Job's life-breath: his own nišmāh and the divine rûaḥ. Both terms connect to Genesis 2:7 where God breathed the 'nišmaṯ ḥayyîm' into the creature. The double reference implies that even Job's physical survival is sustained by divine action — the very God he disputes is the one keeping him alive to make the dispute. This creates the paradox of theodicy: life as both the gift that enables complaint and the affliction that motivates it.
4: 'My lips will not speak wickedness, and my tongue will not utter deception' — the negative oath formula of the wisdom tradition: Psalms 34:13 ('keep your tongue from evil and your lips from deceitful speech'); Psalms 141:3 ('set a guard over my mouth'); Proverbs 8:7-8 (Wisdom's lips speak truth without deceit). The double denial (lips/tongue in parallel) applies the standard body-member rhetoric of Psalms and Proverbs: the two speech organs together represent the totality of verbal expression. Job is claiming pre-emptive fidelity to the wisdom standards he knows the friends expect.
5: 'God forbid that I should agree you are right' (ḥālîlāh lî ʾim-ʾaṣdîq ʾetḵem) — ḥālîlāh is the strongest negative imprecation in Hebrew (literally 'profanation to me if'; translated in KJV as 'God forbid'); it expresses utter repudiation. 'Until I die I will not surrender my integrity' (ʿaḏ-ʾegwaʿ lōʾ-ʾāsîr tummāṯî mimmennî) — tummāh (integrity/wholeness, the character noun from tām; cf. Job 1:1,8 where God describes Job as 'blameless'/tām). Job's integrity is not just a moral quality but his theological identity: surrendering it would mean agreeing to a false confession, which would be the greater wickedness.
6: 'I hold fast to my righteousness and will not release it' — ṣiḏqāṯî heḥĕzaqtî wĕlōʾ ʾarp̄ehā = my righteousness I have grasped and will not let go. The language of 'grasping/holding fast' (ḥāzaq) is the same used for faith-commitment in Isaiah 41:10 ('I will uphold you with my righteous right hand') and Hebrews 10:23 ('hold fast the confession of our hope'). 'My heart does not condemn me for a single day I have lived' (wĕlōʾ-yiḥrap̄ lĕḇāḇî miyāmāy) — ḥārap̄ = to reproach/upbraid; Job's inner tribunal (lēḇāḇ) pronounces him not guilty for the entire span of his life. This is the OT precedent for the NT concept of a conscience not condemning (1 John 3:21).
7: 'Let my enemy be treated as the wicked, and whoever rises against me as the unrighteous' — the imprecation shifts Job's discourse temporarily to the fate of his opponents. In the Psalms of lament, the imprecation against enemies is a standard element (Psalms 35:4-8; Job 109:6-20; Job 137:7-9). The function of the imprecation is not personal vengeance but appeals to divine justice: the psalmist/Job is asking God to act consistently — if the wicked are to be punished, let those who wrongly accuse Job receive what is due the genuinely wicked. The imprecation is simultaneously a profession of innocence.
8: 'For what hope does the godless man have?' (mah-tiqwaṯ ḥānēp̄) — the hope-vocabulary (tiqwāh) that Job applied to himself in 6:8 and 14:19 is here denied to the godless (ḥānēp̄ = the impious/profane one, Job's term for the religious hypocrite in Job 8:13; Job 13:16; Job 17:8; Job 20:5). The juxtaposition is pointed: the godless man's hope is nothing when God removes the soul (nep̄ēš). This logic — earthly accumulation without God is groundless hope — is the theme of Jesus's rich fool parable (Luke 12:16-21) and the wisdom tradition's consistent warning against wealth as primary security.
9: 'Will God hear his cry when trouble comes upon him?' — the question inverts Job's own situation: Job cries to God and is not answered (Job 30:20); the godless man also cries to God and will not be heard. The verse draws a distinction that is crucial for theodicy: both Job and the godless suffer and cry out, but their situations differ in what they have done with God during prosperity. The godless man has not cultivated relationship with God (v10: 'does he call upon God at all times?'), so his cry in adversity falls into the relational void he created.
10: 'Does he take delight in the Almighty?' (ʾim-ʿal-Šaddai yitʿannāg) — ʿānaḡ (to delight in/take pleasure in; used in Psalms 37:4 'delight yourself in the LORD'; Isaiah 58:14 'then you will delight in the LORD'). The delight-in-God vocabulary is the positive content of the fear-of-God framework: wisdom is not merely cognitive acknowledgment but affective relationship, characterized by delight and regular address. 'Does he call upon God at all times?' — the ʿet-kol-ʿēṯ (at all times) of continuous prayer is the wisdom ideal (Psalms 34:1 'I will bless the LORD at all times'; 1 Thessalonians 5:17 'pray without ceasing').
11: 'I will instruct you about the hand of God' (ʾăyaḏ-ʾēl ʾăʾallep̄kem) — ʾālap̄ (to teach/instruct in pi'el; used in Proverbs 22:25 for teaching); the hand of God (yāḏ-ʾēl) as the agent of divine action, both providential care and punitive judgment. Job here claims the teacher's role that the friends have assumed throughout the dialogue: he will give the instruction (tôrāh) that the friends have been trying to give him, but his will be accurate. The verse marks a rhetorical inversion: the accused becomes the instructor of his accusers.
12: 'You yourselves have seen all this; why then have you become so thoroughly empty?' — hēn ʾattēm kullĕḵem ḥăzîṯem = look, all of you have seen; wĕlāmmāh-zeh hebel tihbālû = why then do you engage in complete emptiness? heḇel (vapor/emptiness/vanity, Ecclesiastes' central word) applied to the friends' elaborate theological arguments. The charge is not that they lack data but that their interpretation of data they have both seen is empty — their framework (retributive theology) is vapor that cannot hold the weight of Job's actual case.
13: Verse 13 begins a section (vv13-23) that closely parallels the three friends' descriptions of the wicked man's fate (Eliphaz in Job 15:17-35; Bildad in Job 18:5-21; Zophar in Job 20:4-29). This structural parallel has generated significant debate: some scholars argue the section is misattributed — that it was originally a third speech of Zophar (the missing speech from cycle 3) and was displaced in transmission. Others argue Job is deliberately using the friends' own theological language to describe those who are genuinely wicked (i.e., the friends themselves), turning their framework against them. The text as we have it attributes it to Job.
14: 'If his children multiply, it is only for the sword' — the doom of the wicked man's posterity is the reverse of the Abrahamic blessing-formula: rather than children being 'like the stars of the sky' (Genesis 22:17), the wicked man's children multiply only to die violently. The four judgments on his family (sword, famine, plague, widows' grief) follow the standard OT judgment-sequence: Jeremiah 14:12 and 15:2 use the same four agents (sword/famine/plague/captivity) as the Deuteronomic punishment pattern for covenant violation.
15: 'His survivors will be buried by plague' (śĕrîḏāyw bammāwet yiqqāḇērû) — śārid (survivor/remnant) buried in death, with no mourning widows weeping over them. The absence of mourning is among the most severe covenant-curses (Jeremiah 16:4,6 'they will not be mourned or buried'; Job 22:18-19 for Jehoiakim: 'the burial of a donkey... dragged away and thrown outside Jerusalem'). Proper burial and mourning were essential to ancient Israelite social dignity; their denial was a form of posthumous disgrace.
16: 'Though he pile up silver like dust and lay up clothing as plentiful as clay' — the accumulation imagery (silver/ḵesep̄ and clothing/lĕḇûš) reflects the standard ANE wealth-assessment: silver by weight and textile stores by volume. In ancient economies where currency was pre-monetary, stored silver and textile inventory represented liquid and non-liquid wealth respectively. The 'like dust' (kĕʿāpār) simile for silver quantity hyperbolically inverts the normal value-hierarchy: what is precious becomes as common as dirt.
17: 'The righteous man will wear it, and the innocent will share out the silver' — the transfer of the wicked man's stored wealth to the righteous is a standard wisdom-theme and covenant-blessing formula: Proverbs 13:22 ('the sinner's wealth is stored up for the righteous'); Job 28:8 ('he who increases his wealth by charging interest accumulates it for one who will be kind to the poor'); Job 27:13-17 describes the complete reversal of fortune. The same motif governs Exodus 3:22 (Israel plundering Egypt at the Exodus) as the transfer of stored wealth from the oppressor to the oppressed.
18: 'He builds his house as a moth builds its web, and like the temporary shelter a watchman puts up' — two fragility images: the moth's cocoon (ʿāš = moth; used in Isaiah 50:9; 51:8 for God destroying enemies 'like a garment the moth will eat') and the agricultural watchman's booth (śukkāh = the temporary harvest-season shelter; the same word as the Feast of Tabernacles/Sukkot booths, Leviticus 23:42-43). Both structures are intrinsically impermanent: the moth-web dissolves, the watchman's booth is abandoned after harvest. The wicked man's permanent-looking house has the internal structure of temporary materials.
19: 'The rich man lies down, but is not gathered to his fathers' — the normal Israelite death formula 'gathered to his people/fathers' (ne'esap̄ ʾel-ʿammāyw; Genesis 25:8 for Abraham; 35:29 for Isaac; 49:33 for Jacob) is denied the wicked man. 'He opens his eyes, and is gone' — the sudden disappearance: in one sleep the accumulated wealth and social position have evaporated. The verse uses the minimal narrative contrast (lies down/opens eyes) to enact the sudden reversal: the space between lying down and waking is all the time the divine judgment requires to strip the wicked man.
20: 'Terrors sweep over him like a flood' (tĕśîgēhû kammayim ballāhôṯ) — the word ballāhôṯ (terrors) appears again — it is a key Joban term (Job 9:34; Job 13:21; Job 15:24; Job 18:11,14; Job 24:17; Job 30:15). Job uses it of his own experience and now of the wicked man's fate; the same terrors that assail the innocent Job are presented as the just punishment of the wicked. 'A storm carries him off in the night' — the storm-judgment motif characteristic of ANE theophanic judgment (Amos 1:14; Nahum 1:3; Habakkuk 3:14).
21: 'The east wind sweeps him away and he is gone' — the qādîm (east wind) is the devastating desert sirocco that desiccates crops and humans alike; it is used in prophetic judgment texts as the agent of divine punishment (Isaiah 27:8; Jeremiah 18:17; Ezekiel 17:10; Job 19:12; Hosea 13:15). The east wind as divine weapon is an ANE theological commonplace: in Canaanite mythology the storm-winds served the divine warrior. In Egypt, the east wind brought the locusts of the plague (Exodus 10:13). The wicked man's home from which he is flung (mimmĕqōmô = from his place) is the social position that the judgment dismantles.
22: 'God hurls his power at him without mercy' (wayyaššlēḵ ʿālāyw wĕlōʾ yaḥmōl) — šālak (to throw/hurl, the same word as throwing something aside or casting a stone); ḥāmal (to spare/have compassion). The combination of violent divine action and the explicit denial of mercy marks total judicial abandonment. In the Psalms, God's lack of mercy is the condition of covenant curse: Psalms 109:12 ('let no one extend kindness to him'); Lamentations 2:2,17,21 for the destruction of Jerusalem where God 'did not spare.'
23: 'Men clap their hands at him and hiss him away from his home' — the two gestures of public contempt: ṣāpaq kap̄ (to clap hands; the gesture of contemptuous dismissal in Lamentations 2:15 'all who pass by clap their hands at you, O daughter Jerusalem'; Nahum 3:19 'all who hear the news about you clap their hands') and šāraq (to hiss/whistle in contempt; used in Jeremiah 19:8; Job 25:9,18 and Lamentations 2:15,16 for the same judgment-scenes). The social drama of public humiliation — shaming through gesture — is the final image of the wicked man's fall, completing the arc from private doom (v19-22) to public disgrace.
